concepts.title: Standard equation of hyperbola
concepts.conceptBody:
Standard Equations of Hyperbola 

Let $\text{S}$ be the focus and ZM the directrix of a hyperbola. Since e $>1$, we can divide SZ internally and externally in the ratio e : 1 , let the points of division be $\text{A}$ and $\text{A'}$ as in the figure.

Let $\text{A}{{\text{A}}^{\text{ }\!\!'\!\!\text{ }}}=2\text{a}$ and is bisected at $\text{C}$.

Then, $\text{SA}=$ e. AZ, SA´ = e.ZA´

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ SA}+\text{S}{{\text{A}}^{\text{ }\!\!'\!\!\text{ }}}=\text{e}\left( \text{AZ}+\text{Z}{{\text{A}}^{\text{ }\!\!'\!\!\text{ }}} \right)=2\text{ae}$

i.e., $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }2\text{SC}=2\text{ae}$ or $\text{SC}=\text{ae}$

similarly, by subtraction,

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & \text{S}{{\text{A}}^{\text{ }\!\!'\!\!\text{ }}}-\text{SA}=\text{e}\left( \text{Z}{{\text{A}}^{\text{ }\!\!'\!\!\text{ }}}-\text{ZA} \right)=2\text{e}\cdot \text{ZC} \\ \Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } & 2\text{a}=2\text{eZC}\Rightarrow \text{ZC}=\text{a}/\text{e} \\ \end{array}$

Now, take $\text{C}$ as the origin, $\text{CA}$ as the $\text{x}$-axis, and the perpendicular line $\text{CY}$ as the $\text{y}$-axis. Then, $\text{S}$ is the point (ae, 0) and ZM the line $\text{x}=\text{a}/\text{e}$. Let $\text{P}\left( \text{x},\text{y} \right)$ be any point on the hyperbola.

Then the condition $\text{P}{{\text{S}}^{2}}={{\text{e}}^{2}}$. (distance of $\text{P}$ from ${{\left. \text{ZM} \right)}^{2}}$ gives ${{(\text{x}-\text{ae})}^{2}}+{{\text{y}}^{2}}={{\text{e}}^{2}}{{(\text{x}-\text{a}/\text{e})}^{2}}$

or $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{x}^{2}}{{(1-e)}^{2}}+{{y}^{2}}={{a}^{2}}{{(1-e)}^{2}}$ i.e., $\frac{{{x}^{2}}}{{{a}^{2}}}-\frac{{{y}^{2}}}{{{a}^{2}}\left( {{e}^{2}}-1 \right)}=1$ …(i)

Since e $>1,{{e}^{2}}-1$ is positive. Let ${{a}^{2}}\left( {{e}^{2}}-1 \right)={{b}^{2}}$. Then the equation (i) becomes $\frac{{{x}^{2}}}{{{a}^{2}}}-\frac{{{y}^{2}}}{{{b}^{2}}}=1$.

The eccentricity e of the hyperbola $\frac{{{x}^{2}}}{{{a}^{2}}}-\frac{{{y}^{2}}}{{{b}^{2}}}=1$ is given by the relation ${{e}^{2}}=\left( 1+\frac{{{b}^{2}}}{{{a}^{2}}} \right)$.

Since the curve is symmetrical about the y-axis, it is clear that there exists another focus $\text{S'}$ at $(-$ ae, 0$)$ and a corresponding directrix $Z'M'$ with the equation $x=-a/e$, such that the same hyperbola is described if a point moves so that its distance from $\text{S'}$ is e times its distance from $\text{Z'M'}$.

examples.title: The one which does not represent a hyperbola is
The equation $x^2+4 x y+y^2+2 x+4 y+2=0$ represents
The equation $x^2-16 x y-11 y^2-12 x+6 y+21=0$ represents
